flashcards.front: What is the normal menstrual cycle length range?
flashcards.back: 21–35 days. Average is 28 days. The follicular phase varies; the luteal phase is nearly constant at 14 days.
flashcards.hint: Not everyone has 28 days — the range is wide.
flashcards.explanation: The cycle begins on day 1 of bleeding and ends the day before the next bleed. Cycles shorter than 21 or longer than 35 days may indicate ovulatory dysfunction and warrant evaluation.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In women with irregular cycles (PCOS, hypothalamic amenorrhea), cycle length can range from <21 to >90 days, making ovulation prediction by calendar unreliable — LH testing is essential.

flashcards.front: What does the LH surge trigger, and when does ovulation occur relative to the surge?
flashcards.back: The LH surge triggers follicular rupture (ovulation). Ovulation occurs approximately 36 hours (24–48 hours) after the onset of the LH surge.
flashcards.hint: Surge → wait 36 hours → ovulation.
flashcards.explanation: The LH surge begins when sustained high estradiol from the dominant follicle triggers positive pituitary feedback. This surge initiates oocyte meiosis resumption, prostaglandin synthesis, and follicular rupture. Detecting the surge on an LH strip gives approximately 24–36 hours of lead time.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Ovulation induction with hCG trigger injection mimics the LH surge — ovulation is timed to occur ~36 hours after injection, allowing precisely timed IUI or egg retrieval.

flashcards.front: What is spinnbarkeit and when is it maximal?
flashcards.back: Spinnbarkeit = the ability of cervical mucus to stretch into a thin thread. Maximal at ovulation (6–10 cm stretchability). Estrogen makes mucus thin and watery; progesterone makes it thick and cellular.
flashcards.hint: Spinnbarkeit = stretchiness. Max at ovulation = fertile window.
flashcards.explanation: Cervical mucus assessment is part of the Billings/fertility awareness method. The 'peak day' (clear, slippery, maximally stretchable mucus) correlates with ovulation. Post-ovulation, progesterone from the corpus luteum rapidly thickens mucus, blocking sperm.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Ferning of cervical mucus (arborization pattern on dried slide under microscope) is also maximal at ovulation under estrogen influence. This is the same principle used in ROM assessment.

flashcards.front: What are the three phases of the uterine cycle?
flashcards.back: 1. Menstrual phase (days 1–5): endometrial shedding. 2. Proliferative phase (days 6–14): estrogen-driven endometrial regrowth. 3. Secretory phase (days 15–28): progesterone transforms endometrium for implantation.
flashcards.hint: Shed → Grow (estrogen) → Secrete (progesterone).
flashcards.explanation: The uterine cycle is synchronized with the ovarian cycle: proliferative phase corresponds to follicular phase; secretory phase corresponds to luteal phase. The implantation window (days 20–24) is the optimal secretory-phase moment for blastocyst implantation.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: The secretory endometrium can be visualized on ultrasound as a trilaminar (three-line) or thick, echogenic pattern. Thin endometrium (<7 mm) in the secretory phase may indicate poor uterine receptivity.

flashcards.front: What is the fertilization window (oocyte viability + sperm viability)?
flashcards.back: Oocyte viable: 12–24 hours post-ovulation. Sperm viable in female tract: 3–5 days. Fertile window: 5 days before ovulation through day of ovulation.
flashcards.hint: Egg = short-lived (24h). Sperm = longer (3–5 days). Plan accordingly.
flashcards.explanation: Because sperm survive longer than the oocyte, intercourse before ovulation (when sperm are already in the tube waiting) can result in conception. Intercourse on the day of ovulation + 1 day before = highest probability of conception.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Having intercourse every 1–2 days during the fertile window optimizes conception probability while maintaining adequate sperm count. Daily intercourse may slightly reduce sperm count in some men.

flashcards.front: What is the role of FSH vs LH in follicular development?
flashcards.back: FSH: stimulates granulosa cells to proliferate, produce estradiol (via aromatase), and secrete inhibin B. LH: stimulates theca cells to produce androgens (precursors for estradiol); later triggers ovulation (LH surge) and corpus luteum formation.
flashcards.hint: FSH = granulosa cells (estrogen factory). LH = theca cells (androgen factory) + ovulation trigger.
flashcards.explanation: The two-cell, two-gonadotropin theory: LH acts on theca cells to produce androstenedione/testosterone, which is transported to granulosa cells where FSH-activated aromatase converts these androgens to estradiol. Both FSH and LH are needed simultaneously for estrogen production.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Clomiphene citrate (Clomid) works by blocking estrogen receptors in the hypothalamus and pituitary, preventing negative feedback → FSH rises → follicular recruitment. It does NOT work if FSH is already very high (indicating follicular failure).

flashcards.front: What are the three germ layers and their main derivatives?
flashcards.back: Ectoderm: skin, CNS (brain, spinal cord), PNS. Mesoderm: heart, muscle, bone, kidney, gonads, blood. Endoderm: GI lining, liver, pancreas, lungs, thyroid, bladder.
flashcards.hint: Ecto = outside (skin/nervous). Meso = middle (muscle/bone/organs). Endo = inside (gut/lungs).
flashcards.explanation: Gastrulation at week 3 post-fertilization creates the three germ layers from the bilaminar embryonic disc. All organs are derivatives of these three layers. Neural tube defects = ectoderm failure (neuroectoderm closure defect).
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Knowing germ layer origins helps understand teratogen effects: thalidomide affects mesoderm-derived limb buds (weeks 4–7); isotretinoin affects neural crest cells (ectoderm-derived); rubella affects ectoderm-derived structures (heart, eyes, ears).

flashcards.front: What is Mittelschmerz?
flashcards.back: Mid-cycle unilateral lower abdominal pain/cramping caused by follicular rupture at ovulation. Usually brief (minutes to hours), self-limiting, and on the side of the ovulating ovary.
flashcards.hint: Mittelschmerz = 'middle pain' in German = ovulation pain.
flashcards.explanation: Occurs in approximately 20% of women at ovulation. Caused by the inflammatory response from follicular rupture and release of follicular fluid/blood into the peritoneal cavity, irritating the parietal peritoneum. It can be used as a natural family planning indicator.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Severe mid-cycle pain with signs of hemodynamic instability should raise concern for a ruptured follicular cyst causing hemoperitoneum — not just normal Mittelschmerz.

flashcards.front: What is the blastocyst, and what are its two components?
flashcards.back: The blastocyst is the 5-day-old embryo: a hollow, fluid-filled sphere with two populations: (1) Inner cell mass (ICM/embryoblast) → forms the embryo. (2) Trophoblast (outer layer) → forms the placenta and chorion.
flashcards.hint: Blastocyst = hollow ball. Inside (ICM) = baby. Outside (trophoblast) = placenta.
flashcards.explanation: The blastocyst forms when the morula develops an inner fluid cavity (blastocoel) at day 4–5. After zona hatching (escape from the zona pellucida at day 5–6), the blastocyst can implant. The ICM undergoes further differentiation into the epiblast and hypoblast, then the three germ layers at gastrulation.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: IVF embryo quality grading evaluates ICM quality (A/B/C) and trophoblast quality (A/B/C) separately — both must be high quality for optimal implantation potential.

flashcards.front: When does the neural tube close, and what supplement prevents neural tube defects?
flashcards.back: The neural tube closes by day 28 post-fertilization (approximately 6 weeks gestational age). Folic acid supplementation (400–800 mcg/day) started at least 1 month pre-conception prevents 50–70% of neural tube defects.
flashcards.hint: Neural tube closes day 28 → MUST have folic acid before conception.
flashcards.explanation: The neural tube forms from neuroectoderm rolling and fusing at the dorsal midline. Failure of closure → NTDs: anencephaly (anterior defect), spina bifida (posterior defect). Folic acid is needed before pregnancy because the tube closes before most women know they're pregnant.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: High-risk women (prior NTD pregnancy, diabetes, valproate use, obesity, MTHFR variant) need 4,000 mcg/day folic acid (10× the standard dose). This is a testable clinical distinction.

flashcards.front: What is GnRH and how does it regulate the HPO axis?
flashcards.back: GnRH (gonadotropin-releasing hormone): released in pulses from the hypothalamic arcuate nucleus. Stimulates anterior pituitary to release FSH and LH. Pulsatile secretion is required — continuous GnRH causes receptor downregulation and suppresses FSH/LH.
flashcards.hint: GnRH must be pulsatile to work. Continuous = desensitization = suppression (used therapeutically).
flashcards.explanation: GnRH pulse frequency and amplitude vary across the cycle: faster pulses in follicular phase favor FSH; slower pulses in luteal phase favor LH. Stress, extreme weight loss, and excessive exercise suppress GnRH pulsatility → hypothalamic amenorrhea.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: GnRH agonists (leuprolide/Lupron): initially cause a flare (temporary gonadotropin surge), then profound suppression after 2–4 weeks (downregulation). Used for endometriosis, uterine fibroids, precocious puberty, prostate cancer.

flashcards.front: What are the WHO MEC Category 4 contraindications to estrogen-containing contraceptives?
flashcards.back: VTE history or current DVT/PE; migraine WITH aura; smoking ≥15 cigarettes/day + age ≥35; breastfeeding <6 weeks postpartum; uncontrolled hypertension; ischemic heart disease/stroke history; antiphospholipid antibodies; severe liver disease.
flashcards.hint: VTE, migraine with aura, smoking/age, breastfeeding early, uncontrolled HTN, cardiac/stroke, APS, severe liver.
flashcards.explanation: WHO MEC Category 4 = unacceptable health risk (method should not be used). Estrogen increases coagulation factor production, reduces antithrombin III, and promotes thrombosis — dangerous in all these conditions. Progestin-only methods (mini-pill, Depo-Provera, IUD, implant) can be used in most of these situations.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Migraine WITHOUT aura is MEC Category 2 (benefits usually outweigh risks). Migraine WITH aura is MEC Category 4 (absolute contraindication). The distinction between 'with' and 'without' aura is critical and frequently tested.

flashcards.front: What are the three methods of emergency contraception and their timing?
flashcards.back: 1. Levonorgestrel (Plan B): within 72 hours (effective up to 120h). 2. Ulipristal acetate (ella): within 120 hours (more effective than Plan B, especially >72h). 3. Copper IUD (Paragard): within 5 days — most effective (>99%).
flashcards.hint: Plan B: 72h. Ella: 120h. Copper IUD: 5 days, best.
flashcards.explanation: Plan B primarily delays or prevents ovulation; it does not end an established pregnancy. Ella (selective progesterone receptor modulator) also primarily prevents ovulation but may affect implantation. Copper IUD prevents fertilization by copper ion toxicity to sperm and may also prevent implantation.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Obese women (BMI ≥30) may have reduced efficacy with Plan B (levonorgestrel) due to drug distribution. Ella has better efficacy across all weights. Copper IUD works equally well regardless of weight.

flashcards.front: What are the phases of the ovarian cycle?
flashcards.back: 1. Follicular phase (day 1–ovulation): FSH-driven follicle development. 2. Ovulation (day ~14 in 28-day cycle): LH surge triggers follicular rupture. 3. Luteal phase (ovulation to day 28): corpus luteum produces progesterone.
flashcards.hint: Follicular (FSH, growing) → Ovulation (LH surge, rupture) → Luteal (progesterone, secretory).
flashcards.explanation: The follicular phase begins on day 1 of menstruation and ends with follicular rupture. The luteal phase begins immediately after ovulation and lasts ~14 days regardless of total cycle length. It ends with corpus luteum degeneration (no pregnancy) or hCG rescue (pregnancy).
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Anovulatory cycles (cycle with no ovulation) can still result in menstrual bleeding from estrogen withdrawal, without the progesterone-dominant secretory phase. Chronic anovulation → unopposed estrogen → endometrial hyperplasia risk.

flashcards.front: What is the implantation window and when does it occur?
flashcards.back: The implantation window is the 3–5 day period of maximal endometrial receptivity for blastocyst implantation: approximately days 20–24 of a 28-day cycle (6–10 days after ovulation). Characterized by pinopodes on the endometrial surface.
flashcards.hint: Implantation window = days 20–24. Progesterone-primed endometrium is receptive.
flashcards.explanation: The window is characterized by: mature secretory endometrium (progesterone-primed), expression of adhesion molecules (integrins), formation of pinopodes (surface projections), leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) secretion. A blastocyst arriving too early or too late will not implant.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In IVF frozen embryo transfers, the endometrial preparation protocol (estrogen + progesterone) is designed to recreate the implantation window. Embryo transfer is timed to the appropriate number of days after progesterone start.

flashcards.front: What teratogenic effect does warfarin have in pregnancy?
flashcards.back: Fetal warfarin syndrome: 1st trimester exposure → nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses (chondrodysplasia punctata), CNS abnormalities. 2nd/3rd trimester: CNS hemorrhage, optic atrophy. Use unfractionated heparin or LMWH instead.
flashcards.hint: Warfarin = teratogen in 1st trimester → switch to heparin for pregnancy.
flashcards.explanation: Warfarin crosses the placenta; heparin does not (too large). Warfarin's fetal effects reflect its interference with vitamin K-dependent carboxylation of proteins in developing cartilage and bone, plus potential for fetal hemorrhage.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Warfarin during weeks 6–12 (active organogenesis) carries highest risk for fetal warfarin syndrome. After 12 weeks, the main risk is fetal hemorrhage. Heparin (UFH or LMWH) is preferred throughout pregnancy for all indications.

flashcards.front: What is primary vs secondary amenorrhea?
flashcards.back: Primary amenorrhea: no menarche by age 15 (with normal secondary sexual characteristics) or no menarche within 5 years of thelarche. Secondary amenorrhea: absence of menses for 3 months (regular prior cycles) or 6 months (irregular).
flashcards.hint: Primary = never started. Secondary = was happening, now stopped.
flashcards.explanation: Primary amenorrhea evaluation: assess Turner syndrome (45,X), Müllerian agenesis (MRKH), androgen insensitivity syndrome, hypothalamic/pituitary causes. Secondary amenorrhea evaluation: first rule out pregnancy, then evaluate FSH, LH, prolactin, TSH.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: The most common cause of secondary amenorrhea is pregnancy. Always check β-hCG first in secondary amenorrhea evaluation, regardless of the patient's reported sexual history.

flashcards.front: What is the corpus albicans?
flashcards.back: The fibrous scar tissue that replaces the corpus luteum after it degenerates (when no pregnancy occurs). White, avascular scar that is gradually reabsorbed over months.
flashcards.hint: Corpus luteum → degenerates → corpus albicans = white scar.
flashcards.explanation: Without hCG rescue, the corpus luteum undergoes luteolysis approximately 14 days after ovulation. Progesterone/estrogen fall causes endometrial shedding (menstruation) and the luteal remnant involutes into the corpus albicans.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: The ovary of a postmenopausal woman is small and densely scarred from decades of corpus luteum cycling. Any new ovarian mass in a postmenopausal woman (who should have no new corpus luteum formation) requires urgent evaluation for malignancy.

flashcards.front: What are the two types of trophoblast and their functions?
flashcards.back: Cytotrophoblast (inner): invades decidua, anchors embryo. Syncytiotrophoblast (outer, multinucleated): produces hCG and other hormones, invades spiral arteries to establish uteroplacental circulation, forms the outer surface of chorionic villi.
flashcards.hint: Cytotrophoblast = anchor. Syncytiotrophoblast = hCG factory + blood supply invasion.
flashcards.explanation: Syncytiotrophoblast invasion of maternal spiral arteries is essential for establishing low-resistance uteroplacental blood flow. Failure of this invasion → high-resistance flow → preeclampsia, IUGR. hCG from syncytiotrophoblast is detectable in blood 8–10 days after fertilization.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Preeclampsia is fundamentally a disease of failed spiral artery remodeling (inadequate trophoblast invasion) leading to maternal vascular endothelial dysfunction. The placenta is at the center of preeclampsia pathophysiology.

flashcards.front: What is dysmenorrhea and what causes primary dysmenorrhea?
flashcards.back: Dysmenorrhea = painful menstruation. Primary: no pathological cause; caused by excess prostaglandins (PGF2α and PGE2) from the endometrium → myometrial cramping, vasoconstriction. Treatment: NSAIDs (prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors), OCPs.
flashcards.hint: Primary dysmenorrhea = prostaglandin-mediated cramps. NSAIDs = first-line treatment.
flashcards.explanation: PGF2α causes intense myometrial contractions and uterine artery vasoconstriction → ischemia → pain. NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2), reducing prostaglandin synthesis. Starting NSAIDs 1–2 days before expected menstruation provides better control than starting at pain onset.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Secondary dysmenorrhea = dysmenorrhea WITH a cause: endometriosis, adenomyosis, fibroids, PID, cervical stenosis. Dysmenorrhea that is progressive, worsens over time, or begins in the mid-20s+ warrants evaluation for secondary causes.

flashcards.front: What hormones does the corpus luteum produce?
flashcards.back: Primarily progesterone (dominant). Also estradiol (secondary), inhibin A, relaxin. Regulated by LH from the pituitary (and later by hCG from trophoblast in pregnancy).
flashcards.hint: Corpus luteum = progesterone factory (+ some E2 and inhibin A).
flashcards.explanation: Progesterone from the corpus luteum transforms the endometrium to a secretory state, raises BBT, thickens cervical mucus, suppresses myometrial contractions, and suppresses FSH/LH (negative feedback). Inhibin A (different from inhibin B in follicular phase) from the corpus luteum provides FSH negative feedback in the luteal phase.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Inhibin A is also a component of the maternal serum quad screen (Down syndrome screening) — elevated inhibin A + elevated AFP + low hCG + low estriol = associated with trisomy 18.

flashcards.front: What is the acrosome reaction?
flashcards.back: Release of hydrolytic enzymes (acrosin, hyaluronidase) from the sperm head's acrosome cap when sperm binds to ZP3 of the zona pellucida. These enzymes digest the zona pellucida, allowing sperm penetration.
flashcards.hint: Acrosome = enzyme cap on sperm head. Reaction = releases enzymes to drill through zona pellucida.
flashcards.explanation: The acrosome reaction can only occur after sperm capacitation (a 7-hour process in the female tract that makes the sperm plasma membrane more fluid and allows the acrosome to respond to ZP3 binding). Without capacitation, sperm cannot undergo the acrosome reaction and cannot fertilize.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: ICSI (intracytoplasmic sperm injection) bypasses the acrosome reaction entirely by mechanically injecting sperm into the oocyte cytoplasm. This is why ICSI is effective for sperm with acrosome defects or failed fertilization in conventional IVF.

flashcards.front: What is the menstrual volume of normal menstruation?
flashcards.back: Normal: 30–80 mL per cycle. Abnormal: <30 mL (hypomenorrhea) or >80 mL (menorrhagia/heavy menstrual bleeding). Practical assessment: >1 pad/tampon per hour for 2+ consecutive hours = excessive.
flashcards.hint: Normal: 30–80 mL per cycle. >80 mL = menorrhagia.
flashcards.explanation: Volume assessment by pad/tampon count is imprecise. The Pictorial Blood Loss Assessment Chart (PBAC) provides a standardized visual method. Hemoglobin/hematocrit monitoring reflects cumulative blood loss better than pad count.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Von Willebrand disease (vWD) is the most common inherited bleeding disorder and a frequently missed cause of heavy menstrual bleeding in adolescent and young adult females. Screen with: aPTT, vWF antigen, vWF activity, factor VIII.

flashcards.front: What is the positive feedback mechanism that triggers the LH surge?
flashcards.back: When estradiol is sustained at high levels (>200 pg/mL for >48–50 hours) from the dominant follicle, the pituitary switches from negative to positive feedback, releasing a massive surge of LH that triggers ovulation ~36 hours later.
flashcards.hint: High E2 for >48h = pituitary flip → positive feedback → LH surge → ovulation.
flashcards.explanation: This is the only time in the reproductive cycle when estrogen has positive (rather than negative) feedback. The mechanism is the dominant follicle's signal to the hypothalamus/pituitary that it is mature and ready for ovulation. Without this positive feedback switch, ovulation cannot occur.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Combined OCP estrogen is dosed at a constant low level — never reaching the sustained high level needed for positive feedback. This is why pills suppress ovulation without triggering a surge.

flashcards.front: What is PMDD and how does it differ from clinical depression?
flashcards.back: PMDD = Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder: severe mood symptoms exclusively in the luteal phase (10–14 days before menses), resolving completely within days of menstruation onset. Clinical depression: persistent, not cyclic, does not fully resolve with menses.
flashcards.hint: PMDD = cyclic, resolves with menses. Depression = persistent, no cyclic pattern.
flashcards.explanation: PMDD requires prospective symptom diary documentation for 2 cycles. DSM-5 criteria: ≥5 symptoms (at least 1 mood-related: irritability, mood swings, depression, anxiety), in the week before menses, resolving after menses onset, with significant functional impairment.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: SSRIs work for PMDD via a different (faster) mechanism than for depression — they can be prescribed only during the luteal phase (days 14–28) with onset of effect in the same cycle. This luteal-phase-only dosing is unique to PMDD.

flashcards.front: What is the inner cell mass (ICM) and what does it become?
flashcards.back: The ICM (embryoblast) is the cluster of cells inside the blastocyst cavity. It gives rise to: the embryo proper, the yolk sac, the amnion, and the allantois. The trophoblast (outer layer) becomes the placenta.
flashcards.hint: ICM = baby + yolk sac + amnion. Trophoblast = placenta.
flashcards.explanation: The ICM undergoes differentiation: epiblast → embryo + amnion; hypoblast → primary yolk sac. The epiblast cells then undergo gastrulation to form the three germ layers. The integrity and quality of the ICM are the most critical determinant of embryo developmental potential.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In embryo splitting (natural twinning), the ICM divides. Timing of the split determines chorionicity/amnionicity: early split (days 1–4) → dichorionic-diamniotic twins; days 4–8 → monochorionic-diamniotic; days 8–13 → monochorionic-monoamniotic; >13 days → conjoined twins.

flashcards.front: What is hypothalamic amenorrhea and what causes it?
flashcards.back: Hypothalamic amenorrhea: functional suppression of GnRH pulsatility leading to low FSH, LH, estrogen, and amenorrhea. Causes: extreme weight loss, excessive exercise, caloric restriction, psychological stress (the female athlete triad).
flashcards.hint: Stress/low weight/exercise → GnRH suppression → no FSH/LH → no estrogen → no period.
flashcards.explanation: The hypothalamus detects metabolic insufficiency and stress signals, reducing GnRH pulse frequency. This shuts down the HPO axis — a protective response preventing pregnancy in unfavorable conditions. It is the most common cause of amenorrhea in athletes and women with eating disorders.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: The Female Athlete Triad: energy deficiency (low caloric intake relative to expenditure) + menstrual dysfunction (oligomenorrhea to amenorrhea) + low bone mineral density (from estrogen deficiency). Now recognized as part of Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S).

flashcards.front: What are the effects of progesterone on cervical mucus?
flashcards.back: Progesterone (from corpus luteum, after ovulation) thickens cervical mucus: it becomes viscous, cellular, opaque, and forms a plug — blocking sperm penetration. This is the contraceptive mechanism of progestin-only methods.
flashcards.hint: Pre-ovulation (estrogen) = thin/clear mucus. Post-ovulation (progesterone) = thick/opaque plug.
flashcards.explanation: This is exploited therapeutically: progestin-only methods (mini-pill, Depo-Provera, Mirena IUD) work primarily through this mechanism in many users. Cervical mucus hostility to sperm is a significant contribution to contraceptive efficacy, especially at lower progestin doses that may not fully suppress ovulation.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In fertility treatment, exogenous progesterone used for luteal phase support may thicken cervical mucus — this is not a concern because embryo transfer bypasses the cervix entirely.

flashcards.front: What is the morula and when does it form?
flashcards.back: The morula is a solid ball of 16+ cells formed by days 3–4 post-fertilization as the zygote undergoes cleavage divisions during tubal transport. No increase in overall size (cells get smaller with each division).
flashcards.hint: Morula = mulberry-shaped solid cell ball at days 3–4.
flashcards.explanation: The transition from morula to blastocyst (through cavitation and fluid accumulation creating the blastocoel) occurs at days 4–5. The cells of the morula are totipotent (can become any cell type). Embryo quality at the morula stage affects subsequent blastocyst development.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Cleavage division (mitosis without cell growth) means each daughter cell is half the size of the parent. This is why the morula has the same overall size as the zygote — the zona pellucida constrains expansion until blastocyst zona hatching.

flashcards.front: What is isotretinoin's mechanism of teratogenicity?
flashcards.back: Isotretinoin (retinoic acid derivative) disrupts retinoic acid signaling critical for neural crest cell migration during organogenesis. This causes craniofacial malformations, CNS defects, cardiac anomalies, and thymic hypoplasia with near-universal penetrance.
flashcards.hint: Isotretinoin → blocks normal retinoic acid signaling → neural crest cells fail → craniofacial + CNS + cardiac defects.
flashcards.explanation: Neural crest cells (ectoderm-derived) migrate extensively during embryonic development and contribute to facial bones, the heart (septation), thymus, and cranial nerves. Retinoic acid excess from isotretinoin disrupts this migration. Fetal defects occur in virtually all exposed pregnancies.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: The iPLEDGE program requires continued contraception for 1 month AFTER stopping isotretinoin because the drug has a 10–20 day half-life and tissue levels persist. A negative pregnancy test must be confirmed within 30 days of the last dose.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes primary from secondary dysmenorrhea?
flashcards.back: Primary dysmenorrhea: painful periods with NO pathological cause; onset soon after menarche; caused by prostaglandins. Secondary dysmenorrhea: painful periods WITH a pathological cause (endometriosis, adenomyosis, fibroids, PID, cervical stenosis); often onset later in reproductive years.
flashcards.hint: Primary = prostaglandins, no pathology, young onset. Secondary = has a cause, often progressive.
flashcards.explanation: Primary dysmenorrhea responds well to NSAIDs and OCPs. Secondary dysmenorrhea requires diagnosis and treatment of the underlying cause. Red flags for secondary: onset after age 25, progressive worsening over years, dyspareunia, dyschezia, failed response to NSAIDs/OCPs.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Endometriosis-related dysmenorrhea classically worsens over years and involves pelvic pain extending throughout the cycle (not just menstruation). Deep infiltrating endometriosis causes dyspareunia and dyschezia (painful defecation).

flashcards.front: What happens to the zona pellucida before implantation?
flashcards.back: Zona hatching: the blastocyst secretes zona lysin (hatching protease) that digests a hole in the zona pellucida, allowing the blastocyst to 'hatch out' and expose its trophoblast surface for direct contact with the endometrium. Required for implantation.
flashcards.hint: Zona hatching = blastocyst escapes its shell before implanting. No hatching = no implantation.
flashcards.explanation: In IVF, zona hatching occasionally fails, preventing implantation. Assisted hatching (using laser, acid, or mechanical methods to create a small opening in the zona) is performed before embryo transfer in some cases (older women, thick zona, prior implantation failure) to facilitate hatching.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: After zona hatching, the blastocyst rapidly expands and begins apposition with the endometrial surface. Implantation (apposition → adhesion → invasion) follows within 1–2 days of hatching.

flashcards.front: What is the two-cell, two-gonadotropin theory of estrogen production?
flashcards.back: LH acts on theca cells → androgen production (androstenedione, testosterone). FSH acts on granulosa cells → aromatase enzyme → converts androgens to estradiol. Both cell types and both gonadotropins are required for estradiol production.
flashcards.hint: Two cells: LH → theca (androgens). FSH → granulosa (aromatase → E2). Both needed.
flashcards.explanation: This is why gonadotropin stimulation protocols use both FSH (to stimulate follicle growth and aromatase activity) and LH or hCG (to stimulate androgen substrate production). Without LH activity, granulosa cells lack androgen substrate for conversion.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In PCOS, excess LH drives excess androgen production in theca cells. Insulin (elevated in insulin-resistant PCOS patients) also directly stimulates theca cell androgen production, compounding the androgen excess.

flashcards.front: What is the mid-luteal progesterone test used for?
flashcards.back: Serum progesterone measured 7 days after ovulation (day 21 in a 28-day cycle) to confirm that ovulation occurred. Level >3 ng/mL = ovulation likely; >10 ng/mL = robust corpus luteal function.
flashcards.hint: Day 21 progesterone >3 ng/mL = ovulation confirmed (retrospectively).
flashcards.explanation: The mid-luteal progesterone is collected 7 days after the presumed ovulation day (not necessarily day 21 in women with irregular cycles). In a 35-day cycle (ovulation day 21), the test should be on day 28. For irregular cycles, calculate from the NEXT expected period minus 7 days.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: A low mid-luteal progesterone (<3 ng/mL) indicates anovulation. Values between 3–10 ng/mL suggest ovulation but possible luteal phase defect (inadequate corpus luteum function). Values >10 ng/mL indicate good ovulatory function.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between capacitation and the acrosome reaction?
flashcards.back: Capacitation: 7-hour maturation process in female reproductive tract that makes sperm capable of fertilizing (increased motility, membrane changes). Acrosome reaction: release of hydrolytic enzymes from sperm head when sperm contacts the zona pellucida; enables zona penetration.
flashcards.hint: Capacitation = prerequisites (takes 7h in female tract). Acrosome reaction = zona drilling (happens at moment of zona contact).
flashcards.explanation: Capacitation and acrosome reaction are sequential requirements. Sperm ejaculated directly from semen cannot fertilize (not capacitated). After exposure to the female reproductive tract, they acquire fertilizing capacity. Then, zona contact triggers the acrosome reaction.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In IVF, sperm must be incubated in culture medium to undergo capacitation before insemination with eggs. For ICSI, capacitation is still performed but the acrosome reaction is bypassed by mechanical injection.

flashcards.front: What causes primary dysmenorrhea?
flashcards.back: Excess endometrial prostaglandins (PGF2α and PGE2) during menstruation. PGF2α causes intense myometrial contractions and uterine artery vasoconstriction → ischemia → pain. Higher prostaglandin levels = worse cramps.
flashcards.hint: Primary dysmenorrhea = prostaglandin excess → myometrial spasm + ischemia.
flashcards.explanation: Prostaglandin levels peak in the first 48 hours of menstruation. This explains why dysmenorrhea is worst on days 1–2. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) inhibit prostaglandin synthesis (COX enzyme) → reduce cramps. Starting NSAIDs 1–2 days before expected flow prevents the initial prostaglandin surge.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: High dietary omega-6 to omega-3 ratio (typical Western diet) may increase pro-inflammatory prostaglandins. Fish oil supplementation (omega-3) may reduce dysmenorrhea severity — modest evidence, low risk.

flashcards.front: What is the luteal-placental shift?
flashcards.back: The transfer of progesterone production from the corpus luteum to the placenta at approximately 8–10 weeks gestation. After this shift, the placenta becomes the dominant progesterone source and the corpus luteum is no longer critical.
flashcards.hint: Luteal-placental shift = progesterone handoff from corpus luteum to placenta at 8–10 weeks.
flashcards.explanation: The syncytiotrophoblast progressively develops capacity for progesterone (and estrogen) synthesis from LDL-cholesterol. hCG peaks at 8–10 weeks and then declines as hCG stimulation of the corpus luteum becomes less necessary. If surgical removal of the corpus luteum is needed before this shift, progesterone supplementation prevents miscarriage.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: The luteal-placental shift is also why morning sickness often improves after 10–12 weeks — hCG levels peak then decline, reducing the nausea-stimulating effects.

flashcards.front: What is anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) and when is the best time to test it?
flashcards.back: AMH is produced by granulosa cells of small antral follicles; reflects ovarian reserve (number of remaining primordial follicles). Unlike FSH and estradiol, AMH is cycle-independent — it can be tested on any day of the cycle.
flashcards.hint: AMH = ovarian reserve marker. Can test ANY day of cycle (no need to wait for day 3).
flashcards.explanation: AMH has replaced day 3 FSH in many fertility clinics as the primary ovarian reserve test. Low AMH (<1 ng/mL) indicates diminished reserve. High AMH (>4–5 ng/mL) may indicate PCOS. AMH declines progressively with age, tracking the loss of primordial follicles.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: AMH levels are suppressed by hormonal contraceptives — a woman on OCPs will have a falsely low AMH. Testing should be done after stopping OCPs for at least 1–2 months.

flashcards.front: What happens if polyspermy occurs?
flashcards.back: Polyspermy = fertilization by more than one sperm → triploid embryo (69 chromosomes). Triploid embryos are non-viable and typically result in early miscarriage or partial hydatidiform mole.
flashcards.hint: Polyspermy → 69 chromosomes = triploid = fatal. Partial mole is one result.
flashcards.explanation: Normally, the zona reaction (cortical granule release hardening the zona pellucida) prevents polyspermy within seconds of first sperm entry. If this mechanism fails (zona reaction defect, aged oocyte), multiple sperm may enter → triploid embryo → non-viable or partial mole.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Partial hydatidiform mole is typically 69,XXX or 69,XXY — with two paternal sets of chromosomes and one maternal set (diandric triploidy). This distinguishes it from complete mole (46,XX or 46,XY, entirely paternal).

flashcards.front: What is the follicular phase hormone sequence from day 1 to ovulation?
flashcards.back: Day 1–5: FSH rises (corpus luteum gone, inhibin B falls). Day 5–12: dominant follicle grows → estradiol rises. Day 12–13: estradiol peaks → positive feedback → LH surge begins. Day 14: LH surge peak → ovulation occurs ~36 hours after surge onset.
flashcards.hint: FSH up → follicle grows → E2 up → LH surge → ovulate.
flashcards.explanation: This hormone cascade must proceed in the correct sequence and magnitude for ovulation to occur. Any disruption (elevated prolactin suppressing GnRH, inadequate FSH from OCP use, insufficient E2 from a deficient follicle) can prevent ovulation.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In superovulation protocols for IUI/IVF, exogenous gonadotropins replace this natural cascade — FSH is given daily to grow multiple follicles; when ≥2–3 follicles reach 18+ mm, hCG is given to replace the LH surge and trigger ovulation/retrieval.

flashcards.front: What is the proliferative endometrium?
flashcards.back: The endometrial phase during the follicular phase (days 6–14): rising estrogen from developing follicles stimulates endometrial regrowth from the basal layer. Glands elongate and proliferate; thickness increases from 1–2 mm to 6–10 mm.
flashcards.hint: Proliferative = estrogen growing the endometrium. Think: E for Estrogen = Endometrium rebuilding.
flashcards.explanation: The proliferative phase restores the functional layer shed during menstruation. On ultrasound, proliferative endometrium appears as a trilaminar (three-line) pattern — two outer echogenic lines (endometrial surface) with a hypoechoic functional layer and central echogenic line.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Unopposed estrogen (without progesterone opposition, as in anovulation) causes continuous proliferative stimulation → endometrial hyperplasia → risk for endometrial cancer over time. Annual progestin withdrawal is recommended for women with chronic anovulation.

flashcards.front: What is the secretory endometrium?
flashcards.back: The endometrial phase during the luteal phase (days 15–28): progesterone from corpus luteum transforms the endometrium. Glands coil and fill with glycogen; stroma becomes edematous; thickness 10–14 mm. Creates the implantation-ready environment.
flashcards.hint: Secretory = progesterone-shaped endometrium. Glycogen-filled glands = food for embryo.
flashcards.explanation: The secretory endometrium is visualized on ultrasound as homogeneous and echogenic (bright). The glycogen-rich glandular secretions provide nutrition for the blastocyst before placental blood supply is established. Inadequate secretory transformation → implantation failure.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Endometrial dating (histological assessment of secretory gland morphology) was historically used to diagnose luteal phase defect. It is no longer recommended as a reliable diagnostic tool due to poor inter-observer reliability.

flashcards.front: What is the fertile window relative to ovulation?
flashcards.back: The fertile window spans from 5 days before ovulation to the day of ovulation (6 days total). Peak fertility = 2 days before ovulation + day of ovulation. Outside this window = essentially infertile.
flashcards.hint: Fertile window: 5 days before + day of ovulation = 6 days total. Peak: 2 days before ovulation.
flashcards.explanation: Sperm can survive in the cervical mucus and reproductive tract for up to 5 days in the pre-ovulatory estrogen-rich environment. The oocyte is viable for only 12–24 hours post-ovulation. Having intercourse before ovulation ensures sperm are waiting when the egg arrives.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Natural family planning (calendar method, symptothermal method) relies on identifying and avoiding the fertile window. Failure rates are higher than hormonal methods but can be as low as 1–3% with perfect use of the symptothermal method.

flashcards.front: What is pronuclear fusion (syngamy) and when does it occur?
flashcards.back: Syngamy: the male and female pronuclei fuse following fertilization, creating the zygote with 46 chromosomes. Occurs approximately 20–24 hours after sperm-egg fusion, completing the fertilization process.
flashcards.hint: Pronuclear fusion = final step of fertilization. Male pronucleus + female pronucleus = zygote (46 chromosomes).
flashcards.explanation: Before fusion, each pronucleus contains one complete haploid set (23 chromosomes). After decondensation and DNA replication, the pronuclei migrate toward each other and fuse (syngamy). The first cleavage division follows immediately.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In IVF, fertilization is confirmed at the 2-pronuclear (2PN) stage (~18–20 hours after insemination): both pronuclei are visible under microscopy before fusion. Abnormal: 1PN (parthenogenesis) or 3+PN (polyspermy) → these embryos are not transferred.

flashcards.front: What is the Billings Ovulation Method?
flashcards.back: Natural family planning method based on monitoring cervical mucus changes. Dry days (infertile) → sticky/cloudy mucus (potentially fertile) → peak day (clear, slippery, stretchable = most fertile) → return to sticky/dry (infertile). Avoidance of unprotected sex from first mucus appearance until 3 days after peak day.
flashcards.hint: Peak mucus day = most fertile. Avoid from mucus onset to 3 days after peak day.
flashcards.explanation: The peak day (clear, maximal spinnbarkeit mucus) correlates with the day before, day of, or day after ovulation. Typical use failure rate: 13–23%. Perfect use: 3–5%. Requires training and consistency. Cervical mucus can be affected by infections, semen, and some medications.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: The Billings method and symptothermal method (combining BBT and mucus monitoring) are approved by the Catholic Church as natural family planning. The symptothermal method (combining both) has higher efficacy than either method alone.

flashcards.front: What is the role of inhibin B in the follicular phase?
flashcards.back: Inhibin B is secreted by granulosa cells of growing antral follicles in the early follicular phase. It provides negative feedback to the pituitary, suppressing FSH release. Rising inhibin B → falling FSH → dominant follicle selected (FSH-dependent).
flashcards.hint: Inhibin B (follicular phase) → suppresses FSH → helps select dominant follicle.
flashcards.explanation: As multiple antral follicles grow and produce inhibin B, FSH levels decline. The most FSH-sensitive follicle (the dominant follicle) can continue growing at lower FSH levels; the less sensitive follicles cannot → they undergo atresia. Inhibin B is a clinical marker of ovarian reserve (more antral follicles → more inhibin B → lower FSH on day 3).
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Day 3 FSH >10–12 mIU/mL suggests diminished ovarian reserve (less inhibin B feedback from fewer follicles, allowing FSH to rise). This is the classic fertility workup finding.

flashcards.front: What is Naegele's rule and how does it relate to conception?
flashcards.back: Naegele's rule calculates EDD (estimated due date): First day of LMP + 9 months + 7 days (or LMP + 280 days). This assumes a 28-day cycle with ovulation/conception on day 14.
flashcards.hint: EDD = LMP + 280 days. Conceptional age = gestational age − 2 weeks.
flashcards.explanation: Gestational age (GA) is measured from the first day of the LMP, not from conception. Conception typically occurs ~14 days after LMP (day 14 in a 28-day cycle). So GA is always approximately 2 weeks more than conceptional age. This is why an embryo is '4 weeks' gestational age at the time of implantation but only ~2 weeks old conceptionally.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: For women with cycles longer than 28 days (late ovulation), Naegele's rule overestimates GA. First-trimester ultrasound crown-rump length (CRL) provides the most accurate dating and should be used when there is >5 days discrepancy from LMP dating.

flashcards.front: What is the role of prolactin in relation to the menstrual cycle?
flashcards.back: Elevated prolactin (hyperprolactinemia) suppresses GnRH pulsatility → reduced FSH/LH → anovulation, irregular cycles, or amenorrhea (and galactorrhea). Normal prolactin levels do not significantly affect the menstrual cycle.
flashcards.hint: High prolactin → suppresses GnRH → disrupts cycles → amenorrhea/galactorrhea.
flashcards.explanation: Dopamine normally inhibits prolactin secretion. Hyperprolactinemia causes: amenorrhea-galactorrhea syndrome. Causes: prolactinoma (most common pituitary adenoma), hypothyroidism (elevated TRH stimulates prolactin), dopamine-blocking drugs (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone), chest wall stimulation, pregnancy/breastfeeding.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Breastfeeding-induced hyperprolactinemia provides a natural contraceptive effect (lactational amenorrhea method/LAM): >98% effective for 6 months postpartum in exclusively breastfeeding women with amenorrhea. This is why amenorrhea is common while exclusively breastfeeding.

flashcards.front: What are the phases of spermatogenesis?
flashcards.back: Spermatogonium (stem cell, 2n) → Primary spermatocyte (2n) → [meiosis I] → Secondary spermatocyte (n) → [meiosis II] → Spermatid (n) → [spermiogenesis] → Spermatozoon. Total time: 64–74 days.
flashcards.hint: Spermatogonium → Primary → Secondary → Spermatid → Sperm. Meiosis I and II reduce to haploid.
flashcards.explanation: Spermiogenesis is the final differentiation step where spermatids (round, non-motile) transform into elongated, motile spermatozoa with acrosome, mid-piece (mitochondria), and flagellum. This is distinct from spermatogenesis (the meiotic production phase). After testicular production, epididymal maturation adds 2–12 days.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Because spermatogenesis takes 64–74 days, any insult to the testes (chemotherapy, radiation, fever, orchitis) will not manifest as abnormal semen analysis for 2–3 months. Semen analysis 6–12 months post-insult reflects recovery.

flashcards.front: What is OHSS (ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome) and who is at risk?
flashcards.back: OHSS: excessive response to gonadotropin stimulation → multiple large follicles, elevated estradiol → vascular permeability → fluid shifts (ascites, pleural effusion) → hemoconcentration, thrombosis. High risk: PCOS (high AMH), young age, lean body habitus, prior OHSS, high follicle count.
flashcards.hint: OHSS = ovarian overstimulation → leaky vessels → ascites, hemoconcentration, thrombosis risk.
flashcards.explanation: OHSS occurs when multiple follicles produce very high estradiol levels that trigger VEGF release → capillary leakage. Fluid shifts from intravascular to third space (ascites, pleural effusion). Hemoconcentration increases thrombosis risk (VTE is the most serious complication). Management: monitoring, IV albumin, anticoagulation in severe cases.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: OHSS is triggered/worsened by hCG (trigger shot or from early pregnancy). The 'late OHSS' variant (occurring at 9–14 days post-trigger) is caused by endogenous hCG from the newly implanted embryo and is typically more severe and prolonged.
